Enable the LLM gateway path if Claude on Bedrock, Vertex AI, or Foundry is already routing through your finance approved cloud. Compare review throughput against Microsoft 365 Copilot before standardizing on a sidebar.
items.clientPitch: Anthropic just turned the Microsoft Office stack into a shared Claude workspace. One conversation now spans Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, every change shows up as a tracked redline, and you can route the whole thing through the same Bedrock, Vertex AI, or Foundry contract you already pay for.
items.useCase: Legal contract redlining, financial memo and model alignment, board pack assembly that mixes spreadsheets, slides, and narrative, and any team that already lives in Word and wants AI edits to behave like a human reviewer.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.104 prompts users to approve any tool call that falls outside permission settings
items.description: Anthropic shipped Claude Code 2.1.104 about a day and a half after 2.1.101 with a small but consequential change. Tool calls that fall outside the user's configured permission rules now surface an explicit approve or deny prompt instead of silently failing or running, closing a gap that had been confusing for teams running Claude Code in restricted modes. The release is otherwise tiny at roughly 30 added lines of code and a 1.3 kB bundle delta, with no other user facing additions reported.
items.action: Update Claude Code to 2.1.104 on developer machines and CI runners. Walk through your permission rules or settings.json deny lists and confirm the new approval prompts show up where you expect them, especially for shell commands and file writes. Re record any internal screencasts that demonstrated the old failure path.
items.clientPitch: Claude Code now stops and asks before it runs any tool that your permission rules did not preauthorize. It is a quiet release but it removes an entire category of silent failures and surprise runs in restricted setups.
items.useCase: Engineering teams running Claude Code in regulated or sandboxed environments, plus anyone who maintains shared permission profiles across a team and needs predictable behavior when a tool sits outside the allow list.

2026-04-14.summary: **n8n 2.17.0 wires an AI Gateway, Alibaba Cloud Model Studio, and Moonshot Kimi into the workflow runtime, GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.25 starts installing MCP servers straight from the registry with guided setup, Claude Code 2.1.105 lets PreCompact hooks block compaction and arms background plugin monitors, Vercel AI Gateway adds a one switch Zero Data Retention mode, and Stanford HAI publishes the 2026 AI Index showing China has erased the US model lead.**

items.headline: n8n 2.17.0 adds AI Gateway routing, Alibaba Model Studio node, and Moonshot Kimi chat model
items.description: n8n shipped 2.17.0 on April 13 with AI Gateway support across the AI nodes, a top up flow for gateway credits, and brand new built in nodes for Alibaba Cloud Model Studio and Moonshot Kimi covering both chat and sub agent use. The MCP Client Tool Node now prefixes tool names with their server name, the AI Builder gained a workflow evaluation framework with LLM mock execution and structured sub agent briefings, and operators get expression based role mapping with drag to reorder rules plus configurable VM expression bridge timeout and memory limits. About fifty bug fixes round out the release across AWS DynamoDB auto parsing, Box pagination, Google Drive, HTTP Request, Microsoft Teams, Oracle, and core webhook cleanup.
items.action: Upgrade self hosted n8n to 2.17.0 in a staging instance and point the AI nodes at your existing AI Gateway credentials before rolling to production. Pilot the Alibaba Model Studio and Moonshot Kimi nodes on one workflow each to compare cost and latency against your current OpenAI or Anthropic routes. Audit any custom MCP tool naming since Tool Node names now include the server prefix.
items.clientPitch: n8n now treats AI as a first class part of the runtime. You can route every AI call through one gateway, swap Alibaba and Moonshot models in one click, evaluate AI builder workflows with mock LLMs, and provision roles with expressions instead of console clicks.
items.useCase: Multi provider AI workflow orchestration, cost arbitrage between Western and Chinese model APIs inside a single automation, MCP tool routing in a shared n8n instance, and any workflow where the AI builder needs to be tested before shipping.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.25 installs MCP servers from the registry with guided configuration
items.description: GitHub shipped Copilot CLI 1.0.25 on April 13 with first class MCP registry support: builders can now browse and install MCP servers from the GitHub MCP registry directly in the terminal with a guided setup wizard, and ACP clients can supply their own stdio, HTTP, or SSE MCP servers when starting or loading sessions. The release also adds a /env command that prints the loaded environment including instructions, MCP servers, skills, agents, and plugins, a /remote command and --remote flag for remote controlling CLI sessions, and automatic retry of MCP remote server connections on transient network failures. Custom instruction files now preserve special characters like ampersand and quotes, and the Esc key works correctly after a failed /resume lookup.
items.action: Update Copilot CLI to 1.0.25 on developer machines and CI runners. Replace any hand maintained MCP server install scripts with the new registry guided flow and verify your team allowlist policy still applies. Run /env on a clean session to baseline the loaded MCP servers, skills, and agents before you start layering in remote control via /remote.
items.clientPitch: GitHub just turned the MCP registry into a one command install for Copilot CLI. Your team can discover, install, and configure MCP servers without touching JSON files, and ACP clients can now bring their own MCP servers when they connect to a session.
items.useCase: Standing up MCP server fleets across a developer team without per machine config, enterprises that want a single source of truth for which MCP servers are approved, and remote control of long running CLI sessions from a different machine.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.105 lets PreCompact hooks block compaction and ships a background monitor manifest for plugins
items.description: Anthropic shipped Claude Code 2.1.105 late on April 13 with PreCompact hook support that can now block compaction by exiting with code 2 or returning a JSON decision, plus a top level monitors manifest key that auto arms background monitors when a plugin loads or when a skill is invoked. The release adds a path parameter to the EnterWorktree tool, makes /proactive an alias for /loop, raises the skill description listing cap from 250 to 1,536 characters, strips style and script tags from WebFetch responses, and adds a five minute timeout that retries stalled API streams non streaming instead of hanging. Dozens of fixes follow, including queued message images being dropped, blank screens on long conversations, malformed MCP stdio output hanging sessions, and 429 errors printing raw JSON instead of clean messages.
items.action: Update Claude Code to 2.1.105 on developer machines and any CI runners. If you depend on auto compaction, audit your PreCompact hooks to confirm none accidentally block compaction; if you ship plugins, evaluate adding a monitors manifest entry to replace ad hoc background watchers. Re run any /doctor diagnostics with the new f shortcut to have Claude fix detected issues.
items.clientPitch: Claude Code now lets your hooks veto context compaction and lets plugins arm background monitors at session start without manual setup. The release also stops stalled API streams from hanging and cleans up the 429 error output developers were tripping on.
items.useCase: Long running coding sessions that need surgical control over when context gets compacted, plugin authors building always on observers (lint, test, security), and teams running Claude Code on flaky networks where stalled streams used to require a manual interrupt.

items.headline: Vercel AI Gateway gates traffic to Zero Data Retention providers with a single team setting
items.description: Vercel announced in its April 13 weekly that AI Gateway now offers team wide Zero Data Retention controls so admins can flip a single switch and have every AI request automatically routed only to providers with a Zero Data Retention agreement, removing the need to negotiate per provider opt outs. The same week added Qwen 3.6 Plus, Z.ai GLM 5.1, and Fast mode support for Claude Opus 4.6 to the gateway, and shipped custom class serialization in the Workflow SDK for passing class instances between workflow and step functions. The Chat SDK gained an official Liveblocks vendor adapter for bots that read and respond inside Liveblocks Comments threads.
items.action: If you use Vercel AI Gateway, enable Zero Data Retention at the team level and audit which models become unavailable under the new gating. Roll the new Qwen 3.6 Plus and GLM 5.1 entries into your routing fallback chain. Test Fast mode on Claude Opus 4.6 for any latency sensitive flow.
items.clientPitch: Vercel AI Gateway now lets you guarantee Zero Data Retention with a single team switch instead of chasing legal opt outs across every provider. The same release adds Qwen 3.6 Plus and GLM 5.1 to the gateway and turns on Fast mode for Claude Opus 4.6.
items.useCase: Regulated industries that need contractual ZDR by default, builders who want to add Chinese open weight models to a gateway routing chain, and applications that want sub second Opus 4.6 responses without changing prompts.

items.headline: Stanford HAI publishes the 2026 AI Index showing China has erased the US model lead
items.description: Stanford HAI released the ninth edition of its AI Index on April 13, reporting that the gap between the top US and Chinese frontier models has effectively closed and that as of March 2026 Anthropic's leader sits only 2.7 percentage points ahead of the best Chinese model. The report finds generative AI hit 53 percent global population adoption within three years (faster than the PC or the internet), with Singapore at 61 percent and the United Arab Emirates at 54 percent versus the United States at 28.3 percent in 24th place. The Foundation Model Transparency Index average score collapsed from 58 to 40 as labs disclose less about training data, compute, capabilities, and risks.
items.action: Use the new Index numbers when you scope multi provider sourcing strategies; the China gap close means Qwen, GLM, and DeepSeek are now defensible primaries for non US workloads. Quote the adoption gap when pitching automation projects in Asia and Europe where the curve is six to ten years ahead of the US benchmark. Re check your model risk register against the lower transparency scores.
items.clientPitch: The 2026 Stanford AI Index is out and it confirms what builders already see in benchmarks: Chinese frontier models are within three points of the US leaders, transparency is collapsing, and Asia and Europe are adopting AI faster than North America. It is the cleanest single source of statistics to bring to a strategy conversation.
items.useCase: Anchoring strategy decks with current benchmark numbers, justifying multi provider sourcing that includes Chinese labs, and benchmarking automation adoption rates against country level baselines.

2026-04-15.summary: **Anthropic announces June 15 retirement for Claude Sonnet 4 and Opus 4 while The Information reports a Claude Opus 4.7 plus an AI website and presentation design tool shipping this week, Claude Code 2.1.108 and 2.1.109 add one hour prompt cache TTL, a session recap, and model discovered built in slash commands, GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.26 and 1.0.27 ship /ask plus marketplace refresh and localhost only ACP, n8n 2.17.1 adds a MiniMax chat model sub node, and Google DeepMind opens Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 on the Gemini API.**

items.headline: Anthropic sets June 15 retirement for Claude Sonnet 4 and Opus 4, forcing migration to the 4.6 generation
items.description: Anthropic announced the deprecation of Claude Sonnet 4 (claude-sonnet-4-20250514) and Claude Opus 4 (claude-opus-4-20250514) on April 14, with retirement on the Claude API scheduled for June 15, 2026. The release notes direct every remaining user to Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Claude Opus 4.6 before that cutoff. Workloads still pinned to the 20250514 snapshots will stop working two months from now.
items.action: Audit every Claude API key and Bedrock, Vertex AI, and Foundry workload for the claude-sonnet-4-20250514 and claude-opus-4-20250514 model IDs, then update them to claude-sonnet-4-6 and claude-opus-4-6 in staging this week. Re baseline token cost and latency on the 4.6 generation since pricing and thinking behavior differ from the retiring snapshots. Set a calendar reminder for May 15 to confirm every caller has migrated and another for June 10 to catch any new integrations that slipped the net.
items.clientPitch: Any Claude integration still using the original 4 series models will return errors from June 15. We can audit every client workflow, dual run the 4.6 replacements in staging, and cut over before the retirement window closes so nothing breaks in production.
items.useCase: Migration planning for any n8n, Make.com, Zapier, or custom pipeline that pins a Claude model ID, plus vendor risk sign off for Bedrock and Vertex AI customers that need a documented migration path.

items.headline: Anthropic readies Claude Opus 4.7 and an AI website and presentation design tool for a same week launch
items.description: The Information reported on April 14 that Anthropic is preparing to ship Claude Opus 4.7 alongside a new AI powered tool for designing websites and presentations, with both products expected as soon as this week. The described design tool creates apps, landing pages, photo galleries, and simple games from natural language prompts and bundles live previews, databases, authentication, analytics, and one click deployment. The market reaction was immediate with Figma down 6 percent, Wix down 4.7 percent, Adobe down 2.7 percent, and GoDaddy down 3 percent on April 14.
items.action: Treat Opus 4.7 as the next default in your Claude router as soon as it lands in platform.claude.com and validate any extended thinking or agent team workloads against it before rolling forward. Spin up a sandbox of the new design tool the moment it ships to benchmark it against Figma Make, Lovable, and Vercel v0 for fast client prototype work. Warn clients already on Figma, Wix, or GoDaddy that Anthropic is entering their lane and plan positioning for multi tool stacks.
items.clientPitch: Anthropic is about to ship a flagship model upgrade and a direct competitor to Figma, Wix, and Adobe that turns prompts into live, deployable web apps. We can have a client ready prototype pipeline on the new tool the day it ships so you benefit from the cost drop without rebuilding your design stack.
items.useCase: Client landing page and presentation prototyping straight from a brief, internal tool building without a design system investment, and multi agent long horizon workloads that benefit from the Opus 4.7 intelligence bump.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.108 ships one hour prompt caching, a session recap, and lets the model invoke built in slash commands
items.description: Claude Code 2.1.108 landed April 14 with the ENABLE_PROMPT_CACHING_1H env var that opts API key, Bedrock, Vertex, and Foundry users into a one hour cache TTL, a new /recap slash command that rebuilds context when returning to a session, and a Skill tool path that lets the model discover and invoke built in /init, /review, and /security-review without the user running them. The release also turns /undo into an alias for /rewind, adds warnings when /model switches mid conversation and when prompt caching is disabled, filters /resume to the current directory by default, and reduces memory for file reads and edits through on demand syntax grammar loading. Version 2.1.109 shipped hours later on April 15 with a rotating progress hint on the extended thinking indicator.
items.action: Export ENABLE_PROMPT_CACHING_1H=1 in any shared Claude Code runner whose prompts are reused between 5 minutes and 1 hour, and set FORCE_PROMPT_CACHING_5M only where ephemeral workloads need the short window. Wire /recap into your session onboarding guide since returning users now get automatic context instead of a cold transcript. Audit any policy that blocked slash commands, since the model can now invoke /security-review and /review itself via the Skill tool.
items.clientPitch: Claude Code sessions that span a work day just got dramatically cheaper because the cache now survives lunch breaks instead of expiring in five minutes, and returning developers now get an automatic recap so they skip the ramp up.
items.useCase: Long running Claude Code sessions in consulting engagements, cost optimization for teams paying per token on Bedrock or Vertex AI, and enforcing automatic security review on every significant code change.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.26 binds ACP to localhost and 1.0.27 adds a /ask command and a marketplace refresh
items.description: GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.26 shipped April 14 with eighteen improvements including ACP server binding to localhost only to prevent unintended network exposure, session stability when context compaction splits tool calls at checkpoints, plugin hook environment variables, MCP retry logic, and a fix for slash prefixed tokens being treated as file paths. Version 1.0.27 followed on April 15 with a /ask command for quick questions that do not touch conversation history and a copilot plugin marketplace update command that refreshes plugin catalogs without reinstalling. WSL clipboard paste no longer injects invisible BOM characters and the status bar now hints @files, #issues, and slash commands while typing.
items.action: Upgrade Copilot CLI on every developer and CI runner to 1.0.27 so the localhost ACP binding and the context compaction stability fix ship together, and rerun any automation that depended on the old plugin marketplace sync. Add the new /ask command to onboarding docs so quick questions stop polluting the main conversation thread. Re test any WSL setup that previously had paste corruption issues before closing related bugs.
items.clientPitch: Copilot CLI now keeps quick questions out of long running conversations, refreshes plugin marketplaces with a single command, and closes a network exposure gap on its agent protocol server. These changes matter for any team running Copilot CLI on shared developer machines or inside CI.
items.useCase: Shared dev containers where the ACP port used to be reachable by other processes, long context compaction heavy coding sessions that were crashing under 1.0.25, and plugin distribution via internal marketplaces.

items.headline: n8n 2.17.1 pre release adds a MiniMax chat model sub node and 2.16.1 ships a stable line patch
items.description: n8n tagged 2.17.1 on April 15 as a pre release on top of the 2.17.0 AI Gateway drop from two days earlier, adding a MiniMax chat model sub node (pull request 28485) that can now be used anywhere n8n expects an AI chat model or sub agent. The stable 2.16.1 patch went out the same day and 1.123.31 carried a maintenance fix to the 1.x branch. Together the three tags let both the bleeding edge pre release line and the two stable channels pick up MiniMax coverage and rollup fixes on the same day.
items.action: Pilot the MiniMax chat model sub node on one workflow in a staging 2.17.1 instance and benchmark it against your existing OpenAI, Anthropic, and Alibaba Model Studio routes for cost, latency, and Mandarin quality. Promote the 2.16.1 patch to any production self hosted stack that has been holding on 2.16.0. Upgrade self hosted 1.x instances to 1.123.31 even if you are still on the old major.
items.clientPitch: n8n now covers every major Chinese frontier model provider out of the box. You can mix MiniMax, Moonshot Kimi, Alibaba Cloud Model Studio, and the Western labs in one workflow, with price arbitrage taken in the UI instead of in code.
items.useCase: Cost arbitrage between MiniMax and Western APIs inside a single n8n workflow, sub agent orchestration where the planner and executor use different providers, and localization pipelines that need a native Chinese language model.

items.headline: Google DeepMind opens Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 on the Gemini API with instrument reading jumping from 23 to 93 percent
items.description: Google DeepMind released Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 on April 14 and made it available to developers immediately through the Gemini API and Google AI Studio. The embodied reasoning model lifts analog gauge and instrument reading accuracy from 23 percent on the 1.5 version to 93 percent using agentic vision and improves pointing, counting, and success detection over both 1.5 and Gemini 3.0 Flash. Boston Dynamics Spot is already using it to read dashboards autonomously.
items.action: Evaluate Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 only if your pipeline needs vision grounded spatial reasoning on physical instruments such as meters, gauges, and dashboards, since this is a specialized robotics model rather than a general language update. For general multimodal work stay on Gemini 3.0 Flash or wait for the next flagship. Keep a note that the ER series now crosses the 90 percent gauge reading bar in case a client opportunity in manufacturing, utilities, or field service arrives.
items.clientPitch: Google just made it possible to read a physical gauge or meter from a photo with near perfect accuracy through a standard API call. For clients in manufacturing, energy, or field service this unlocks automation of readings that used to require an on site worker.
items.useCase: Automated meter and gauge reading in utilities, quality control checks on industrial equipment, and robotics planning that needs success detection after an action.

2026-04-16.summary: **OpenAI ships GPT-5.4-Cyber through an expanded Trusted Access for Cyber program to answer Anthropic Mythos, Anthropic rebuilds the Claude Code desktop app around parallel sessions and adds scheduled cloud Routines, Anthropic moves Claude Enterprise to flat per seat plus metered compute billing, OpenAI Codex CLI 0.121.0 stable ships plugin marketplace installs, Claude Code 2.1.110 adds a fullscreen TUI and a push notification tool, and Google releases a native Gemini macOS app with a global Option+Space shortcut.**

items.headline: OpenAI launches GPT-5.4-Cyber and scales Trusted Access for Cyber to thousands of vetted defenders
items.description: OpenAI shipped GPT-5.4-Cyber, a cyber permissive variant of GPT-5.4 tuned for defensive security with added binary reverse engineering capability, and rolled it into a tiered Trusted Access for Cyber program open to thousands of verified individual defenders and hundreds of security teams protecting critical software. Individuals verify at chatgpt.com/cyber while enterprises request access through an OpenAI representative, with higher tiers requiring customers to waive Zero Data Retention so OpenAI can monitor usage. The move directly answers Anthropic Mythos with a broad verified access model instead of Anthropic's tightly gated approach.
items.action: If you run a SOC, pentesting practice, or vulnerability research team, apply for Trusted Access for Cyber now to get early seats on GPT-5.4-Cyber. Audit ZDR commitments in your enterprise contracts before accepting higher tier access, and model the split sourcing pattern of broad OpenAI Cyber access alongside narrow Anthropic Mythos seats for the same defensive workloads.
items.clientPitch: Builders of defensive security tooling now have two philosophically opposed frontier options. OpenAI Cyber will be available to thousands of verified defenders while Anthropic Mythos stays locked to 11 elite partners. That means your cyber automation stack can now draw on restricted frontier capability without negotiating a bespoke preview seat.
items.useCase: SOC automation, binary reverse engineering for malware analysis, vulnerability research workflows that previously tripped safety refusals, red team exercises for internal systems, and government or regulated enterprise security teams that need vetted frontier model access.

items.headline: Anthropic rebuilds Claude Code desktop around parallel sessions and ships scheduled, API, and GitHub triggered Routines
items.description: Anthropic released a redesigned Claude Code desktop app for Mac with multiple parallel sessions per window, an integrated terminal, file editor, HTML and PDF preview, and a faster diff viewer in a drag and drop layout. Alongside it, the company shipped Routines in research preview: saved configurations that bundle a prompt, one or more repositories, and connectors, then run on Claude Code web infrastructure on a cron schedule, from a dedicated HTTP endpoint, or in response to GitHub events, all without the user's laptop needing to be online. Pro users get 5 routine runs per day, Max gets 15, and Team and Enterprise get 25.
items.action: Install the new Claude Code desktop build on Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise accounts and move your multi repo orchestration workflows into parallel sessions. Replace any lightweight cron driven Claude Code shell scripts with Routines, and wire up API triggered Routines to incoming webhooks from n8n, Zapier, or Make so business events can kick off Claude Code runs directly.
items.clientPitch: Claude Code now runs scheduled automations in the cloud. That removes the laptop dependency that previously kept it out of production, and the GitHub trigger mode means PR events can spin up isolated Claude sessions without any custom infrastructure. This is the closest Anthropic has come to shipping a managed agent service inside the consumer plans.
items.useCase: Nightly code review jobs, scheduled documentation refreshes, GitHub PR triage agents, API triggered customer ticket handlers, and parallel multi repo refactors that benefit from the new desktop side by side session layout.

items.headline: Anthropic moves Claude Enterprise to a flat $20 per seat plus metered compute billing model
items.description: Anthropic confirmed a pricing overhaul for Claude Enterprise that replaces the previous up to $200 per licensed seat plan with a flat $20 per user per month base fee plus consumption based charges for compute capacity used. Previous seats came with a set allowance of discounted tokens, while the new model shifts most spend onto metered usage. Reports indicate the change is expected to double or triple costs for heavy power users while lowering entry costs for lightly used seats.
items.action: Pull the last 30 days of Claude Enterprise token usage from the Usage and Cost API and rebuild your TCO model against the new $20 per seat plus compute pricing before your renewal. Identify power users whose consumption would push them into the doubled or tripled cost bracket and consider routing them through prompt caching, compaction, or Haiku 4.5 tiered workflows to contain spend.
items.clientPitch: Claude Enterprise is no longer a predictable flat seat cost. Finance teams need to model the new $20 per seat floor plus metered compute, and builders need to aggressively adopt 1 hour prompt caching and compaction to keep the bill under control. Clients that did not track per user consumption yesterday will need to start today.
items.useCase: Finance and procurement planning for Claude Enterprise renewals, cost allocation between teams and projects, and optimization of token heavy workflows such as long context code review, document processing, and agentic pipelines.

items.headline: OpenAI Codex CLI 0.121.0 ships stable with plugin marketplace installs, reverse history search, and sandbox fixes
items.description: OpenAI cut the Codex CLI 0.121.0 stable release after nearly two weeks of alpha builds, adding `codex marketplace add` with app server support for installing plugin marketplaces directly from GitHub and a TUI reverse history search. The release also lands macOS sandbox handling fixes, Windows path compatibility improvements, and app server plumbing for remote control workflows. An immediate 0.122.0 alpha branch was opened the same day, signalling continued rapid iteration.
items.action: Upgrade local and CI Codex CLI installs to 0.121.0, then publish any internal plugin bundles through the new marketplace add path instead of hand rolled install scripts. If you run Codex on Windows or macOS sandbox profiles, retest your pipelines against the fixes before the next release train.
items.clientPitch: Codex CLI now treats third party plugin marketplaces as a first class install surface, matching the MCP registry path in GitHub Copilot CLI. That makes it realistic to distribute client specific Codex agents through internal GitHub repos and to have Codex self install them on new workstations.
items.useCase: Internal Codex plugin distribution, templated developer onboarding on macOS and Windows, and agent driven CI workflows that previously tripped over sandbox edge cases.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.110 adds fullscreen TUI, a mobile push notification tool, and expanded Remote Control command reach
items.description: Anthropic shipped Claude Code 2.1.110 with a new `/tui` command and `tui` setting that renders the terminal UI in flicker free fullscreen mode, plus a push notification tool that fires mobile alerts through Remote Control with an optional `Push when Claude decides` toggle. The release also swaps Ctrl+O to toggle between the normal and verbose transcript, adds a `/focus` view toggle, lets `/autocompact`, `/context`, `/exit`, and `/reload-plugins` run from Remote Control clients, and resurrects unexpired scheduled tasks on `--resume` and `--continue`. A thin 2.1.109 release earlier the same day added a rotating progress hint to the extended thinking indicator.
items.action: Upgrade to 2.1.110 and turn on `/tui fullscreen` for long running Claude Code sessions where scrollback flicker has been a friction point. Pair the push notification tool with mobile Remote Control so long Routines and scheduled tasks can interrupt you when they need input instead of silently stalling.
items.clientPitch: Claude Code just gained a real fullscreen terminal UI and mobile push alerts. That means a developer can kick off a long Claude Code run from a desktop, walk away, and get notified on phone when a plan needs approval, without wiring up any external notification service.
items.useCase: Long agent runs that need human approval gates, mobile first developers running Remote Control from the road, and CI-adjacent Claude Code workflows that previously had no way to surface attention requests.

items.headline: Google ships native Gemini macOS app with global Option+Space shortcut, screen sharing, and file analysis
items.description: Google released a native Gemini app for macOS, available free for all Gemini users on macOS 15 and later running Apple Silicon. The app adds a global Option+Space shortcut for summoning Gemini anywhere on the desktop, window level screen sharing for contextual questions, and built in image generation, screen analysis, and file review. Gemini is the last of the three major AI assistants to ship a dedicated Mac app, closing a long standing gap with ChatGPT and Claude.
items.action: Install the Gemini Mac app on workstations that already use Gemini API credits to move ad hoc document analysis and image generation off the browser. If you deploy managed macOS fleets, stage the download from gemini.google/mac and lock the global shortcut through MDM so it does not clash with internal productivity hotkeys.
items.clientPitch: Gemini now sits natively next to Claude and ChatGPT on macOS. For clients that have standardised on Google Workspace but bounced between Gemini browser tabs, this removes a daily friction point and opens the door to full screen share assisted support.
items.useCase: Ad hoc Gemini usage inside Google Workspace centric teams, contextual troubleshooting through screen sharing, and file grounded drafting or analysis that previously required uploading files through the web UI.

2026-04-17.summary: Anthropic ships Claude Opus 4.7 as the most capable generally available model on the Claude API, Bedrock, Vertex AI, and Foundry with a new tokenizer that forces a cost and migration review, Claude Code 2.1.111 lands xhigh effort plus auto mode for Max subscribers, the European Parliament votes to delay high risk AI Act obligations by more than a year, and GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.29 adds native Opus 4.7 routing.

items.headline: Anthropic launches Claude Opus 4.7 with a new tokenizer, xhigh effort, and breaking changes versus Opus 4.6
items.description: Anthropic made Claude Opus 4.7 generally available on the Claude API, Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Vertex AI, and Microsoft Foundry at the same $5 per million input and $25 per million output pricing as Opus 4.6. A rebuilt tokenizer produces roughly 1.0x to 1.35x more tokens for the same prompts, so same price per token translates into an effective cost increase on existing workloads. Opus 4.7 adds an xhigh effort level, task budgets for agent loops, 3.75 megapixel image support, and removes Extended Thinking Budgets, certain sampling parameters, and assistant prefill.
items.action: Before migrating, rerun the messages count_tokens endpoint on representative prompts to forecast the token bump, raise max_tokens headroom and compaction triggers, and drop any prefill or manual thinking budgets from your Opus 4.6 code paths. In Claude Code run /claude-api migrate to rewrite projects to claude-opus-4-7 automatically.
items.clientPitch: The most capable generally available Anthropic model just shipped at unchanged list pricing but with a tokenizer that raises the effective bill by up to 35 percent and breaks prefilled assistants. Any Opus 4.6 workload needs a migration review this week, not next quarter.
items.useCase: Long horizon coding agents, complex document and image analysis over 3.75 megapixel scans and PDFs, and multi step workflows that previously hit Opus 4.6 quality ceilings and can now exploit the xhigh effort and task budget primitives.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.111 ships xhigh effort slider, Max tier auto mode on Opus 4.7, and a parallel agent ultrareview command
items.description: Anthropic shipped Claude Code v2.1.111 alongside the Opus 4.7 launch, exposing the new xhigh effort level through an interactive /effort slider and making auto mode available to Max subscribers on Opus 4.7 without the enable-auto-mode flag. The release adds /ultrareview for multi agent code review, a /less-permission-prompts skill that scans transcripts to populate settings allowlists, a progressive PowerShell tool rollout on Windows toggled by CLAUDE_CODE_USE_POWERSHELL_TOOL, and plan files now named after the originating prompt. Hotfix v2.1.112 the same day resolves a claude-opus-4-7 temporarily unavailable error in auto mode.
items.action: Upgrade Claude Code to 2.1.112 before routing Opus 4.7 traffic through auto mode, switch long session effort to xhigh via the slider, and run /less-permission-prompts on recent transcripts to shrink the approval surface in team settings.
items.clientPitch: Claude Code is now the reference harness for Opus 4.7, with the new effort ladder, parallel review agents, and PowerShell support for Windows engineering teams that were previously stuck with bash only workflows.
items.useCase: Max tier engineering teams delegating deep reviews and architecture passes to Opus 4.7, Windows first shops adopting Claude Code without WSL, and projects trimming permission prompts to streamline autonomous runs.

items.headline: European Parliament votes to delay core AI Act obligations into late 2027 and 2028
items.description: MEPs approved a compromise with the Commission that pushes application of the high risk AI system rules from August 2, 2026 to December 2, 2027, and for AI systems tied to sectoral safety legislation to August 2, 2028. The watermarking and content provenance obligations for generative providers move to November 2, 2026 rather than August. The package still needs Council of the European Union sign off, targeted before June, for the delay to take legal effect before the original deadline.
items.action: Do not pause your AI Act readiness work, the original August 2, 2026 dates remain enforceable until the Council confirms. Use the new calendar to sequence watermarking first, then high risk system conformity, and revisit EU deployment contracts that referenced the 2026 dates.
items.clientPitch: Europe just gave AI builders a real breather on the hardest compliance work but only if the Council approves the delay in the next eight weeks. Treat the new dates as a working target, not a guarantee.
items.useCase: EU facing AI deployments in critical infrastructure, education, employment, and law enforcement that were previously racing an August 2026 deadline, and generative content workflows planning watermarking controls.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.29 through 1.0.31 add Opus 4.7 routing, statusline command, and image clipboard paste
items.description: GitHub shipped three Copilot CLI releases on April 16 bringing native Claude Opus 4.7 support, a /statusline command to customize the status bar, and restored Ctrl+V and Meta+V image clipboard paste across platforms. Remote MCP server configuration now permits omitting the type field, session environment variable COPILOT_AGENT_SESSION_ID is forwarded to shell commands and MCP servers, and rendering issues on Windows and Ubuntu prompt frames are fixed. Skills plugin discovery and git remote repository owner parsing also got targeted fixes.
items.action: Upgrade Copilot CLI to 1.0.31 to pick up the Windows and Ubuntu frame fix before any demos, switch default model to claude-opus-4-7 if you standardized on Anthropic, and audit custom MCP server configs to take advantage of the relaxed type field.
items.clientPitch: GitHub is tracking Anthropic releases within hours, Copilot CLI now speaks Opus 4.7 the same day as Claude Code, so there is no longer a tooling gap between the two major coding agents on the Anthropic stack.
items.useCase: Developer teams standardized on Copilot CLI who want Opus 4.7 without switching harnesses, Windows shells that previously showed distorted prompt frames, and agents wiring remote MCP servers with minimal config.

2026-04-18.summary: Anthropic launches Claude Design, an AI design tool powered by Opus 4.7 that generates prototypes and slide decks from prompts and sent Figma shares down 7 percent, OpenAI ships native sandbox execution and a model harness into the Agents SDK with seven third party sandbox providers, Claude Haiku 3 retires tomorrow, and Google previews Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS with 200 plus audio tags across 70 languages.

items.headline: Anthropic launches Claude Design, an AI prototyping and visual design tool that sent Figma shares down 7 percent
items.description: Anthropic released Claude Design on April 17, a new product powered by Claude Opus 4.7 that lets users generate prototypes, slide decks, marketing one pagers, and app mockups from text prompts. During onboarding Claude Design reads a team's codebase and design files and builds a persistent design system of colours, typography, and components that it applies to every subsequent project. Refinement happens through chat, inline comments, direct text editing, and custom adjustment sliders that Claude itself generates.
items.action: Evaluate Claude Design against current Figma and Canva workflows for internal slide decks, client pitch decks, and landing page mockups. If your team already pays for Claude Pro or Max the feature is included at no extra cost, so test it on a real deliverable this week.
items.clientPitch: Anthropic just shipped an AI design surface that auto reads your codebase and produces brand consistent prototypes, decks, and one pagers from a prompt. Figma dropped 7 percent on the news. For teams already on Claude Pro or above this is a zero marginal cost addition to the stack.
items.useCase: Rapid client deliverable generation, internal deck creation, app prototype iteration without a dedicated designer, and brand consistent marketing collateral for teams that already use Claude for code and copy.

items.headline: OpenAI Agents SDK ships native sandbox execution and a model harness with seven third party providers
items.description: OpenAI released a major Agents SDK update adding native sandbox execution and a model native harness. The sandbox lets agents run in controlled environments with file and tool access, with built in support for Blaxel, Cloudflare, Daytona, E2B, Modal, Runloop, and Vercel, or bring your own provider. The harness adds configurable memory, standardised integrations, portable workspace support, and built in snapshotting with rehydration so agent runs survive container loss.
items.action: If you build OpenAI agents, migrate from custom sandbox wrappers to the native SDK sandbox. Pick a provider that matches your existing infra. Review the Assistants API migration guide since OpenAI confirmed a formal deprecation with a mid 2026 target sunset.
items.clientPitch: OpenAI just made agent sandboxing a first class SDK primitive instead of a DIY stack. Seven providers are supported out of the box, agent state survives crashes via checkpointing, and the Assistants API is now on a confirmed deprecation path toward mid 2026.
items.useCase: Enterprise agent deployments that need isolated execution, file system access, and durability. Any workflow automation agent that currently uses a custom sandbox layer can now standardise on the SDK primitive and reduce maintenance overhead.

items.headline: Claude Haiku 3 retires tomorrow and all API requests to the model will begin returning errors
items.description: Anthropic's Claude Haiku 3 model (claude-3-haiku-20240307) reaches its retirement date on April 19, 2026. After retirement all API requests to this model ID will return errors. Anthropic announced the deprecation on February 19 with the recommended migration path to Claude Haiku 4.5 (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001).
items.action: Search your codebase and automation configurations for any reference to claude-3-haiku-20240307 or the claude-3-haiku alias today. Replace with claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 or the claude-haiku-4-5 alias. Test before tomorrow's cutoff.
items.clientPitch: If any client integration still points at Claude Haiku 3 it will break tomorrow. Haiku 4.5 is a direct replacement at the same speed and cost tier with better capability. This is a zero excuse migration that needs to ship today.
items.useCase: Any production pipeline, automation, or integration that sends API requests to the claude-3-haiku-20240307 model ID, including Make.com and n8n HTTP request nodes with hardcoded model strings.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.113 switches to a native binary, adds domain deny lists, and hardens sandbox security
items.description: Claude Code v2.1.113 shipped April 17 with a structural change: the CLI now spawns a native per platform binary instead of bundled JavaScript. A new sandbox.network.deniedDomains setting blocks specific domains even when a broader allowedDomains wildcard would otherwise permit them. Security hardening treats macOS /private paths as dangerous removal targets, matches Bash commands wrapped in env, sudo, watch, and similar exec wrappers against deny rules, and stops find -exec and find -delete from auto approving under Bash(find:*) allow rules.
items.action: Update Claude Code to 2.1.113. Review any custom Bash allow rules that relied on find:* auto approving exec or delete subcommands. If you use sandbox.network.allowedDomains, consider pairing it with deniedDomains to block specific hosts within a broad wildcard.
items.clientPitch: Claude Code moved to a native binary and closed several sandbox bypass vectors including exec wrapper evasion and find command auto approval. If you run Claude Code in production or client environments the security hardening alone justifies the update.
items.useCase: Agentic coding sessions in restricted enterprise environments, CI pipelines that grant Claude Code limited tool permissions, and any deployment where sandbox domain control needs to block specific endpoints within a broader allow policy.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.32 adds automatic model selection, usage limit warnings, and document file attachments
items.description: GitHub Copilot CLI v1.0.32 shipped April 17 with an auto model selector that routes prompts to the best available model without manual configuration. Usage limit warnings now fire at 75 and 90 percent thresholds so teams see consumption before they hit the wall. Document file attachment lets agents reason over uploaded files, and a new --connect flag enables direct remote session connection without the picker flow.
items.action: Update to Copilot CLI 1.0.32. Try the auto model selector by passing --model auto and compare output quality versus your current pinned model. If your team runs close to usage limits the new 75 percent warning gives lead time to adjust plans before throttling hits.
items.clientPitch: Copilot CLI now picks the best model automatically, warns before you hit rate limits, and lets agents read attached documents. The auto model selector alone removes a recurring decision point for teams that previously pinned a specific model and missed capability improvements.
items.useCase: Developer teams using Copilot CLI for code generation, code review, and documentation tasks where automatic model routing reduces configuration overhead and the usage warnings prevent surprise throttling mid sprint.

items.headline: Google previews Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS with 200 plus audio tags, 70 languages, and top Elo on the TTS leaderboard
items.description: Google released Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS in preview on the Gemini API, Google AI Studio, and Vertex AI on April 15. The model introduces over 200 audio tags that control vocal style, pace, and delivery by embedding natural language commands directly into text input across more than 70 languages. On the Artificial Analysis TTS leaderboard it reached an Elo score of 1211, overtaking ElevenLabs. All generated audio is watermarked with SynthID.
items.action: Test Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS via the Gemini API for any voice AI pipeline or IVR workflow. Compare latency and quality against your current ElevenLabs or Cloud TTS provider. The 200 plus audio tags give fine grained style control that may eliminate post processing steps.
items.clientPitch: Google's new TTS model topped the public leaderboard on day one and supports 200 plus inline style tags across 70 languages. For any client running voice workflows, IVR, or audio content generation this is worth a head to head test against the incumbent provider.
items.useCase: Voice AI pipelines, interactive voice response systems, audio content generation, multilingual customer support agents, and any workflow that currently calls a separate TTS API and could consolidate onto the Gemini API.

2026-04-20.summary: Claude Haiku 3 retired yesterday with all API requests now returning errors, Cloudflare shipped its Agents Week infrastructure stack with AI Gateway routing 70 plus models across 14 providers through a single endpoint, and Zhipu AI's GLM-5 leaves NVIDIA NIM today with its replacement not yet available.

items.headline: Claude Haiku 3 retired yesterday and all API requests to the model now return errors
items.description: Anthropic's Claude Haiku 3 model (claude-3-haiku-20240307) reached its retirement date on April 19. All API requests to this model ID now return errors instead of completions. The migration target is Claude Haiku 4.5 (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001), which offers a 64K output window up from 4K, extended thinking support, and the same speed tier at $1/$5 per million tokens input/output.
items.action: Search all codebases, automation platforms, and integration configs for claude-3-haiku-20240307 or the claude-3-haiku alias immediately. Any reference that was not migrated before yesterday is now producing errors in production. Replace with claude-haiku-4-5-20251001.
items.clientPitch: If any client system still references Claude Haiku 3 it is broken right now. The migration to Haiku 4.5 is a direct swap with better capability at the same speed tier. Any pipeline that hardcodes the old model string needs an emergency update today.
items.useCase: Any production pipeline, Make.com scenario, n8n workflow, Zapier Zap, or custom integration that sends API requests to the claude-3-haiku-20240307 model ID. Also applies to Bedrock and Vertex AI deployments referencing the retired model.

items.headline: Cloudflare Agents Week ships AI Gateway with 70 plus models across 14 providers and a single API endpoint
items.description: Cloudflare's Agents Week (April 13 to 17) delivered a full agent infrastructure stack. AI Gateway now routes to 70 plus models across 14 providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Groq, xAI, Alibaba Cloud, and others through one API endpoint with unified billing and one line model switching. The week also shipped Dynamic Workers (isolate based runtime 100x faster than containers for agent code execution), Sandboxes GA (persistent isolated environments with shell and filesystem), Cloudflare Mesh for agent to agent communication, Agent Memory, Browser Run, and Artifacts (git compatible versioned storage in private beta).
items.action: Evaluate Cloudflare AI Gateway as a unified inference layer if you currently manage separate API keys and billing across multiple LLM providers. The single endpoint with automatic failover, spend monitoring, and one line model switching directly simplifies multi provider orchestration in Make.com and n8n HTTP request nodes.
items.clientPitch: Cloudflare just shipped the infrastructure layer for running agents at scale: one API endpoint for 70 plus models across 14 providers with unified billing, millisecond cold start sandboxes, and git compatible artifact storage. For any client running multi provider AI workloads this collapses API key sprawl, billing fragmentation, and failover logic into a single managed service.
items.useCase: Multi provider LLM routing in production automations, agent sandboxing without container overhead, unified AI spend tracking across OpenAI and Anthropic and Google, and durable agent state with Artifacts for workflows that need versioned output storage.

items.headline: Zhipu AI GLM-5 deprecated on NVIDIA NIM today with the GLM-5.1 replacement not yet available on the platform
items.description: Zhipu AI's GLM-5, a 744B mixture of experts model, reaches its deprecation date on NVIDIA NIM on April 20. The recommended successor GLM-5.1 offers stronger coding and agentic capabilities but was flagged by developers on the NVIDIA forums as not yet available through the NIM API at the time of the deprecation announcement, creating a potential coverage gap for teams that built on GLM-5 through NVIDIA's inference platform.
items.action: If you route inference through NVIDIA NIM and use GLM-5, check whether GLM-5.1 is now live on the platform. If not, switch to an alternative model on NIM or route GLM-5.1 requests through another provider until NVIDIA resolves the availability gap.
items.clientPitch: GLM-5 is gone from NVIDIA NIM today and the replacement may not be live yet. Any workflow routing through NIM for GLM-5 inference needs an immediate fallback plan, either GLM-5.1 if it has landed or an alternative model on the same platform.
items.useCase: Any inference pipeline using GLM-5 through NVIDIA NIM API, particularly teams that chose GLM-5 for its mixture of experts efficiency in agentic and reasoning tasks. Also relevant for teams evaluating Chinese foundation models through NVIDIA's managed platform.

2026-04-21.summary: Systemic MCP STDIO design flaw exposes 150 million downloads to arbitrary code execution as Anthropic declines to fix the protocol, while Moonshot AI open sources Kimi K2.6, a trillion parameter open weight agentic model topping GPT-5.4 on coding benchmarks.

items.headline: Systemic MCP STDIO design flaw exposes 150 million downloads to remote code execution as Anthropic declines to fix the protocol
items.description: OX Security disclosed that Anthropic's official MCP SDK contains a fundamental design flaw in the STDIO transport: the server launch command executes regardless of whether the target process starts, allowing any attacker who can influence MCP configuration to run arbitrary OS commands across all supported SDK languages including Python, TypeScript, Java, and Rust. The exposure spans an estimated 200,000 vulnerable servers and over 150 million downloads, with 14 assigned CVEs and more than 30 confirmed RCE issues affecting LiteLLM, LangFlow, Windsurf, Cursor, Flowise, DocsGPT, and GPT Researcher. Anthropic confirmed the behavior is by design and declined to modify the protocol, shifting sanitization responsibility entirely to individual developers and third-party vendors.
items.action: Audit every MCP STDIO transport integration in your stack immediately. Block public IP access to MCP servers, run MCP services inside network-isolated sandboxes, monitor tool invocations for unexpected OS commands, and treat all external MCP configuration input as untrusted. Check whether LiteLLM, Flowise, DocsGPT, and other affected tools in your stack have released patched versions.

items.headline: Moonshot AI open sources Kimi K2.6 with 300 agent swarms and 4000 coordinated steps topping GPT-5.4 on agentic coding benchmarks
items.description: Moonshot AI released Kimi K2.6, a 1 trillion parameter mixture of experts model, as open weight under a Modified MIT License with weights published on Hugging Face and API access at platform.moonshot.ai using an OpenAI-compatible interface. The model's defining capability is hierarchical agent swarm orchestration: a single task can spawn up to 300 specialized sub-agents executing across 4,000 coordinated steps, enabling days-long autonomous execution on complex engineering goals without human checkpoints. On SWE-Bench Pro, K2.6 scores 58.6 ahead of GPT-5.4 xhigh at 57.7 and Claude Opus 4.6 max effort at 53.4, while remaining freely deployable under permissive open weight terms.
items.action: Pull Kimi K2.6 weights from Hugging Face for self-hosted agentic coding pipelines or test the API at platform.moonshot.ai using the OpenAI-compatible interface with no configuration changes. Benchmark K2.6 against your current models for long-horizon tasks where autonomous multi-step execution matters more than single-turn accuracy.

2026-04-22.summary: Google renames Vertex AI to Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and turns any REST API into an MCP server via Apigee at Cloud Next, OpenAI ships gpt-image-2 to the API, and Anthropic signals a Claude Code pricing restructure.

items.headline: OpenAI ships gpt-image-2 with reasoning and 2K resolution to the API for all paid plans
items.description: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Images 2.0 on April 21, powered by the new gpt-image-2 model available now via the API. The model reasons before drawing, producing up to ten images per request at 2K resolution with sharp multilingual text, infographics, maps, and web search integration. API pricing is approximately $0.21 per standard 1024 by 1024 image, with input text at $5 per million tokens and output image tokens at $30 per million.
items.action: Replace gpt-image-1 with gpt-image-2 in any image generation pipeline. Test multilingual text-in-image and multi-output requests, which are now reliable for the first time. Add thinking mode for layout-heavy use cases where plan quality matters.

items.headline: Google renames Vertex AI to Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform with full agent orchestration stack
items.description: Announced at Google Cloud Next 2026, Vertex AI is now the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, Google's unified surface for building, scaling, governing, and optimising agents. The platform ships Agent Studio (low-code visual builder), Agent Development Kit with graph-based sub-agent networks and multimodal streaming, Agent Memory Bank for persistent long-term context, Agent Gateway for secure unified connectivity, and Agent Anomaly Detection. All future Vertex AI services will be delivered exclusively through this platform, with Model Garden hosting 200-plus models including Claude Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku.
items.action: Existing Vertex AI API calls continue working. Evaluate the Agent Development Kit if you currently use LangGraph, CrewAI, or Semantic Kernel, as Google now ships native integrations. Check docs.cloud.google.com/gemini-enterprise-agent-platform for the migration guide and updated API references.

items.headline: Anthropic briefly pulls Claude Code from twenty dollar Pro plan then reverts, signalling a pricing restructure is coming
items.description: On April 22, Anthropic's pricing page removed Claude Code from the $20 Pro plan for approximately two percent of new prosumer signups, reverting the change after developer pushback the same day. Anthropic's head of growth confirmed the intent: "the way people actually use a Claude subscription has changed fundamentally" since the Max plan launched and Opus 4 shipped. The current plan structure was not designed for current usage volumes, and Anthropic said it is evaluating new options.
items.action: Existing Pro and Max subscribers are not affected today. If you are planning new Claude Code deployments or seats, budget for a likely move of Claude Code to Max only ($100 per month) or a new per-seat pricing tier. Avoid committing teams to Pro-tier Claude Code contracts until the official restructure is announced.

items.headline: Apigee becomes MCP bridge translating any REST API into a discoverable agent tool without code changes
items.description: Google announced at Cloud Next 2026 that Apigee, its API management platform, can now translate any existing REST API into a fully governed MCP server. No code changes are required to existing APIs; Apigee uses existing OpenAPI specs and handles transcoding, infrastructure, and lifecycle automatically. All 30-plus Apigee security and governance policies apply to MCP tool calls, including OAuth, rate limiting, and threat detection.
items.action: If your organisation manages REST APIs through Apigee, you can expose them as MCP tools to agents immediately with no redevelopment. For teams not yet on Apigee, this significantly changes the cost-benefit calculation since MCP server management overhead disappears. Review the Cloud API Registry for discoverable trusted MCP tools.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.116 and 2.1.117 ship faster resume, concurrent MCP, and default high effort on Opus and Sonnet 4.6
items.description: v2.1.116 (April 20) cuts /resume time by up to 67 percent on sessions over 40MB, adds inline thinking progress labels, and improves MCP startup with deferred resource loading. v2.1.117 (April 22) sets concurrent MCP server connections as the default, bumps the default effort level to high for Pro and Max subscribers on Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6, loads agent frontmatter mcpServers for main-thread sessions, and replaces the separate Glob and Grep tools with embedded bfs and ugrep in native builds.
items.action: Update to 2.1.117 for automatic effort and MCP improvements. If you run Claude Code with large session histories, the /resume improvement alone is worth the upgrade. Check that any agent frontmatter mcpServers config is correct now that it is loaded for main-thread sessions.

items.headline: OpenAI Codex CLI 0.122.0 stable adds TUI side conversations, Plan Mode carry forward, and deny-read filesystem policies
items.description: The first stable Codex CLI release since 0.121.0 shipped April 20 with a more self-contained standalone installer, TUI side conversations for asking quick questions without interrupting the main thread, Plan Mode that can start implementation in a fresh context, and filesystem deny-read glob policies for managed deny-read requirements. Plugin workflows now include tabbed browsing, inline enable/disable toggles, and multi-source marketplace support.
items.action: Update to 0.122.0 if running Codex CLI in CI or restricted environments. The deny-read glob policies enable locking down filesystem access without abandoning the tool entirely. Test the TUI side conversation feature to reduce context switching during long agentic runs.

items.headline: Agent2Agent protocol reaches v1.2 with cryptographic agent identity at 150 organisations in production
items.description: At Google Cloud Next 2026, A2A reached version 1.2 under Linux Foundation governance with signed agent cards using cryptographic signatures for domain verification. The protocol is running in production across 150 organisations including Microsoft, AWS, Salesforce, SAP, and ServiceNow. Native A2A support is now built into Google's Agent Development Kit, LangGraph, CrewAI, LlamaIndex Agents, Semantic Kernel, and AutoGen.
items.action: A2A now has sufficient adoption that agent-to-agent communication across vendor boundaries is a real architectural option. If you build multi-agent systems spanning platforms, evaluate A2A as the transport layer. The cryptographic agent cards in v1.2 address identity spoofing concerns that previously made cross-org A2A risky to deploy.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.33 adds slash command picker, command aliases, and usage warnings at fifty and ninety-five percent capacity
items.description: Released April 20, the stable 1.0.33 build adds a slash command picker with suggestions, command aliases for repeated operations, and usage warnings at 50 and 95 percent of session capacity so long runs do not fail silently at the limit. Resumed sessions now automatically inherit the --remote flag, and vim-style j/k navigation and task management are included.
items.action: Update to 1.0.33 if running Copilot CLI for agentic sessions. The capacity warning at 95 percent is directly useful for long document or codebase analysis runs where hitting a silent limit costs a full run. Aliases reduce repetitive scaffolding in daily workflows.

items.headline: xAI begins billing for file and collection storage with Collections API search at $2.50 per thousand queries
items.description: Starting April 20, files and collections stored on the xAI platform are charged based on the amount of storage used. The Collections API, which allows uploading and searching PDFs, spreadsheets, and codebases as searchable knowledge bases, is now priced at $2.50 per 1,000 search queries after a free first week for newly uploaded data.
items.action: Audit any files uploaded to the xAI API platform and delete data you no longer need to avoid unexpected charges starting now. If you are building knowledge-base workflows on top of xAI Collections, factor $2.50 per thousand searches into cost modeling versus alternatives like Pinecone or native vector search.

2026-04-23.summary: OpenAI launches persistent Workspace Agents for ChatGPT Business and Enterprise teams on April 22, Google makes BigQuery directly queryable by any MCP agent at GA, and Claude Code gains MCP tool invocation from hooks.

items.headline: OpenAI launches Workspace Agents in ChatGPT, letting teams run persistent Codex-powered agents around the clock
items.description: Workspace Agents are shared, always-on agents that teams on ChatGPT Business, Enterprise, Edu, and Teachers plans can create and configure to handle long-running tasks autonomously in the cloud. They are an evolution of GPTs powered by Codex and can write code, prepare reports, and respond to messages without requiring a human in the loop. Free to use until May 6, 2026, with credit-based pricing starting that date.
items.action: Evaluate Workspace Agents for clients already on ChatGPT Business or Enterprise before the May 6 billing start. This is a direct alternative to standalone automation tools for teams already paying for ChatGPT seats.

items.headline: Google makes BigQuery Remote MCP Server generally available and ships Data Agent Kit for Claude Code, Codex, and VS Code
items.description: Announced at Google Cloud Next 2026 on April 22, BigQuery Remote MCP Server reaches GA, allowing any MCP-compatible agent to query BigQuery directly without custom connectors. The Google Cloud Data Agent Kit (Preview) is a portable suite of MCP tools and skills that integrates into VS Code, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and Codex, automatically selecting the right framework such as BigQuery, dbt, Apache Spark, or Airflow and generating production-ready code. Spanner, AlloyDB, and Cloud SQL MCP support also launched in preview alongside Looker MCP.
items.action: Replace custom BigQuery connectors in agent pipelines with the managed MCP server. Pilot the Data Agent Kit in Claude Code or Codex for any client with data engineering workflows running on Google Cloud.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.118 adds MCP tool invocation from hooks, custom themes, Vim visual modes, and unified usage command
items.description: The April 23 release adds hooks that can now invoke MCP tools directly using type: mcp_tool, enabling hooks to trigger external services as part of automated workflow steps. Other additions include Vim visual mode and visual-line mode with full selection and operator support, named custom themes editable as JSON in ~/.claude/themes/, the /usage command consolidating /cost and /stats, and a DISABLE_UPDATES env var for stricter update blocking. WSL on Windows can now inherit Windows-side managed settings.
items.action: Update to 2.1.118 and test MCP tool hooks in your most complex Claude Code pipelines. The hook-to-MCP bridge opens up new automation patterns that were previously only possible through shell exec workarounds.

items.headline: OpenAI Codex CLI 0.123.0 stable adds built-in Amazon Bedrock provider and MCP verbose diagnostics
items.description: Version 0.123.0, released April 23, ships a built-in Amazon Bedrock model provider with AWS profile support, letting teams route Codex through Bedrock's compliance and access controls without a separate adapter. The enhanced /mcp verbose command provides comprehensive server diagnostics for debugging MCP integrations. Improved realtime handoffs allow background agents to receive transcript updates mid-task.
items.action: Test the --provider=bedrock flag if any clients require Codex in AWS-controlled or regulated environments. The Bedrock route also opens native SageMaker and IAM integration for enterprise deployments.

2026-04-24.summary: OpenAI ships GPT-5.5 to ChatGPT and Codex with API access announced at double GPT-5.4 pricing, Anthropic adds filesystem-backed memory to Claude Managed Agents, and OpenAI open-sources an on-device PII detection model under Apache 2.0.

items.headline: OpenAI releases GPT-5.5 with 1M context window and API pricing set at double GPT-5.4 rates
items.description: GPT-5.5 launched April 23 for ChatGPT Plus, Pro, Business, Enterprise, and Codex users, with API access described as coming very soon at $5 per 1M input tokens and $30 per 1M output tokens, exactly double GPT-5.4 pricing. A premium gpt-5.5-pro tier is priced at $30 per 1M input and $180 per 1M output. OpenAI argues the effective cost is neutral or lower than GPT-5.4 because the model completes the same Codex tasks with fewer tokens due to significantly higher token efficiency. The model includes a 1M context window and enhanced coding capabilities.
items.action: Benchmark GPT-5.5 on your actual production prompts before assuming the cost is comparable. Token efficiency claims depend heavily on task type. Evaluate gpt-5.5-pro only for tasks where GPT-5.4 quality is already a bottleneck. Monitor API availability date, which has not been specified beyond 'very soon'.

items.headline: Anthropic ships filesystem-backed memory for Claude Managed Agents in public beta
items.description: As of April 23, the managed-agents-2026-04-01 beta header now activates persistent memory for Claude Managed Agents. Memory is stored as files on a mounted filesystem so agents can read and write their own knowledge between sessions using the same bash and code execution tools already available in the harness. Files are exportable and manageable via the API, giving builders full control over what each agent retains without building a separate vector store or external database.
items.action: Extend any existing Managed Agents integration to use the memory layer before building a custom persistence layer. The filesystem model means you can inspect, edit, and version agent memory with standard file tools, which is a significant ops advantage over embedding-based memory systems.

items.headline: OpenAI releases Privacy Filter as open-weight Apache 2.0 model for on-device PII detection
items.description: OpenAI published Privacy Filter on April 22, an open-weight 1.5B-parameter sparse model with 50M active parameters per forward pass, designed for high-throughput PII detection and redaction directly on device. It achieves a 96% F1 score across eight privacy categories including names, addresses, emails, phone numbers, and account numbers, and processes long inputs in a single pass without sending data to any API. The model is available under Apache 2.0 on Hugging Face and GitHub, allowing commercial use and fine-tuning without royalties or copyleft requirements.
items.action: Add Privacy Filter as a preprocessing step in any pipeline where user-provided text reaches a cloud model. The on-device execution model means sensitive data can be sanitized before it crosses a trust boundary, which is a compliance win for healthcare, legal, and finance clients.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.119 adds config persistence, multi-platform PR review, and hooks timing data
items.description: Released April 23 at 23:24 UTC, v2.1.119 makes /config settings persist to ~/.claude/settings.json with a full project/local/policy override precedence chain. The --from-pr flag now accepts GitLab, Bitbucket, and GitHub Enterprise PR URLs in addition to GitHub.com, and a new prUrlTemplate setting lets teams point code-review links at any host. Hooks now receive duration_ms in PostToolUse and PostToolUseFailure events, enabling timing-based automation and alerting. PowerShell tool commands can be auto-approved in permission mode on Windows.
items.action: Upgrade to 2.1.119 if your team uses GitLab or Bitbucket for code review, as --from-pr now works natively across platforms. Add duration_ms logging to PostToolUse hooks to identify slow tool calls in long agentic sessions.

items.headline: OpenAI Codex CLI 0.124.0 adds TUI reasoning controls, multi-environment app-server, and Bedrock auth
items.description: Released April 23 at 18:29 UTC, v0.124.0 stable introduces keyboard shortcuts for controlling reasoning depth directly in the TUI without editing config, support for multiple named environments in app-server sessions so teams can switch contexts within one session, and AWS Bedrock authentication for OpenAI-compatible provider configurations. Hooks can now be configured inline as stable config rather than external scripts, and eligible ChatGPT plans default to the Fast service tier.
items.action: Use the multi-environment flag in app-server sessions to separate dev, staging, and production contexts in long-running Codex agents without restarting the session. Test inline hook configuration for simpler CI pipeline integration.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot CLI v1.0.35 stable adds named sessions and slash command argument tab-completion
items.description: Released April 23 at 23:24 UTC, v1.0.35 stable makes slash commands fully tab-completable including arguments and subcommands, which significantly reduces typing in long sessions. Sessions can now be named with --name and resumed by name, replacing the previous UUID-based resume flow. Ctrl+Y is added as an alternative to Tab for accepting highlighted options in completion popups.
items.action: Adopt --name for sessions in shared team environments where multiple engineers hand off the same context. Slash command tab-completion makes the tool meaningfully faster in complex multi-step agent interactions.

2026-04-25.summary: DeepSeek launches V4 Pro and Flash as MIT-licensed open-weight models matching closed frontier performance at one-sixth the cost, GPT-5.5 API goes live for developers, and Claude Code 2.1.120 ships a CI-ready ultrareview subcommand.

items.headline: DeepSeek releases V4 Pro and Flash as MIT-licensed open weights rivaling GPT-5.5 and Opus 4.7 at a fraction of cost
items.description: DeepSeek open-sourced V4-Pro (1.6T-parameter MoE, 49B active per token) and V4-Flash (284B total, 13B active) simultaneously on April 24 under the MIT license with weights published to Hugging Face. Both models expose a 1M-token context window natively, dual Thinking and Non-Thinking modes, and support both OpenAI and Anthropic API message formats out of the box. V4-Pro scores 80.6% on SWE-bench Verified at $1.74/$3.48 per million tokens; V4-Flash scores 79.0% at $0.14/$0.28, roughly one-sixth the cost of Opus 4.7 or GPT-5.5.
items.action: Test V4-Flash as a drop-in for high-volume agent loops where GPT-5.4 Mini or Gemini Flash served before. V4-Pro for near-frontier reasoning at significantly reduced cost. Migrate off deepseek-chat and deepseek-reasoner before their July 24, 2026 retirement.

items.headline: GPT-5.5 API goes live for all key holders one day after ChatGPT-only rollout
items.description: OpenAI confirmed on April 24 that GPT-5.5 and GPT-5.5 Pro are now accessible via standard API keys, closing the gap from the day-prior launch that was limited to ChatGPT and Codex. Standard pricing is $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens; GPT-5.5 Pro is $30/$180. The updated system card describing API-specific safeguards was published alongside the availability announcement. The 1M context window and agentic computer-use capabilities are fully available to API callers.
items.action: Integrate GPT-5.5 into pilot workflows now that API access is live. At double GPT-5.4 pricing, benchmark quality gains against cost before swapping model IDs in production.

items.headline: Claude Code 2.1.120 ships CI-ready ultrareview subcommand and drops Windows Git Bash requirement
items.description: Released April 25 at 00:14 UTC, v2.1.120 adds the `claude ultrareview [target]` subcommand for non-interactive code review that prints findings to stdout and exits with a standard exit code, enabling direct CI pipeline integration. Windows installations no longer require Git for Windows as PowerShell is now the fallback shell tool. Skills can reference the current effort setting via `${CLAUDE_EFFORT}`, and the `AI_AGENT` environment variable is now propagated to subprocesses enabling GitHub traffic attribution to Claude Code.
items.action: Add `claude ultrareview` to your PR CI step for automated code review output. Update effort-sensitive Skills to use `${CLAUDE_EFFORT}`. Windows-only deployments can now drop Git for Windows as a prerequisite.

items.headline: Anthropic launches Rate Limits API for programmatic workspace quota monitoring
items.description: As of April 24, the Rate Limits API lets organization administrators query current rate limit configurations for their org and individual workspaces via a dedicated API endpoint. The response includes both Anthropic-set limits and any custom limits configured in the Console, removing the need for manual Console polling in capacity planning and alerting pipelines. The API is authenticated with standard API keys.
items.action: Wire the Rate Limits API into your observability stack. Replace manual Console checks with automated rate-limit headroom monitoring to catch capacity constraints before they affect production workflows.

items.headline: Codex CLI 0.125.0 adds Unix socket app-server transport and persistent permission profiles
items.description: Released April 24 at 18:00 UTC, v0.125.0 brings stable app-server integrations using Unix socket transport for local inter-process communication without HTTP overhead. Permission profiles now persist across sessions so tool-access approvals survive process restarts and do not require re-granting on each launch. Model provider configuration is also updated in this release.
items.action: Upgrade Codex CLI to 0.125.0 if building local agent harnesses or app-server integrations. The persistent permission profile meaningfully reduces friction for multi-session developer workflows.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot CLI 1.0.36 adds keep-alive command and fixes silently broken custom instruction loading
items.description: Released April 24 at 18:39 UTC, v1.0.36 adds the `/keep-alive` command to maintain session context, introduces a double-Esc requirement to cancel in-progress work preventing accidental interruptions, and fixes a bug where custom instruction files stored in `.gitignored` directories were silently skipped and never applied. Subcommand picker selection indicators were also added.
items.action: Update to 1.0.36 immediately if you use custom `.claude` instruction files in gitignored directories, as those files have been silently failing to load in prior versions.

2026-04-26.summary: Gemini adds autonomous research agents with MCP support to its API; an LLM deployment toolkit sees active SSRF exploitation within hours of disclosure; OpenAI releases GPT-5.5 prompting guidance for API developers.

items.headline: Gemini adds Deep Research Max with MCP support and private data access to Interactions API
items.description: Google launched two new agent models via the Gemini API Interactions API on April 21: Deep Research (speed-optimized) and Deep Research Max (maximum comprehensiveness), both built on Gemini 3.1 Pro. The agents combine web search with private enterprise data sources via MCP servers in a single API call, returning fully cited reports with embedded charts. Access is via paid Gemini API tiers using model IDs deep-research-preview-04-2026 and deep-research-max-preview-04-2026.
items.action: Explore the Interactions API for automated research pipelines; test MCP connector integration to route private data sources alongside web search for enterprise knowledge workflows.

items.headline: LMDeploy SSRF flaw exploited within 13 hours of disclosure affecting VLM inference servers
items.description: CVE-2026-33626, a high-severity (CVSS 7.5) server-side request forgery vulnerability in LMDeploy vision-language image loader, was disclosed April 21 and actively exploited within 13 hours. Attackers used the load_image() function to probe internal networks, cloud metadata services, Redis, and MySQL from VLM inference endpoints. All LMDeploy versions prior to 0.12.3 are affected.
items.action: Upgrade LMDeploy to 0.12.3 immediately. Add network-level controls restricting outbound HTTP from VLM inference servers to known-safe hosts.

items.headline: OpenAI publishes GPT-5.5 prompting guide as API goes live
items.description: OpenAI published dedicated GPT-5.5 prompting guidance on April 25, the day after the API opened to all key holders. The key directives: treat GPT-5.5 as a new model family requiring a fresh baseline rather than a drop-in replacement; use shorter outcome-oriented prompts; avoid over-specified process instructions that narrow the model search space.
